codeReview.summary: Solid implementation covering all feature requirements. NextAuth.js credentials provider configured for email/phone login, register API with bcrypt hashing and comprehensive validation, login page with Phone/Email tabs and password toggle, register page with confirm password, middleware for protected routes, and TypeScript type extensions. 18 tests (8 API, 10 component) cover the required behaviors well. A few non-blocking concerns noted: (1) missing SessionProvider wrapper in app layout — signIn() works without it for direct API calls but useSession() won't work for future features; (2) email validation in register route uses simple includes('@') check which could accept malformed emails; (3) the phone regex /^\+?[\d\s-()]+$/ is quite permissive and would match strings like '---' or '()'; (4) register route returns user.id in response body which could be an information disclosure concern in stricter security contexts. None of these are blocking for the current feature scope.

issues.description: No SessionProvider from next-auth/react wrapping the app. The signIn() function works without it (makes direct API calls), but useSession() and getSession() on the client side will not work. Future features needing client-side session access will require adding SessionProvider. This should be addressed before the next feature that uses session state in client components.

Email validation uses emailOrPhone.includes('@') which is very permissive — strings like 'a@b' or '@@@' would pass as valid email. Consider using a basic email regex pattern for stricter validation. The phone regex /^\+?[\d\s-()]+$/ is also permissive and would match strings like '---' or empty parens '()'. Not blocking since server-side Prisma constraints (unique email/phone) provide a safety net.

Response includes user.id in the 201 response body. While not a security vulnerability in this context (the id is a cuid, not sequential), some security practices recommend not exposing internal IDs in registration responses. Acceptable for this project scope.

NEXTAUTH_SECRET uses process.env.NEXTAUTH_SECRET which could be undefined. The .env file has it set and .env.example documents it, but there's no runtime check or fallback. NextAuth will use a generated secret in dev but this would fail silently in production. Acceptable for this project (local SQLite, no production deployment).

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker did not add a SessionProvider to the app layout. AGENTS.md mentions 'NextAuth.js (credentials provider)' under Auth but doesn't specify that SessionProvider is required for client-side session access. This should be documented as a convention — either that SessionProvider is needed or that client components should use server-side session checking via getServerSession instead.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/layout.tsx and components/LayoutWrapper.tsx — neither wraps children in SessionProvider. Login/register pages use signIn() directly which works without it, but any future component using useSession() will fail.

sharedStateObservations.observation: No input validation library is used — all validation is hand-written in the register route handler. AGENTS.md doesn't specify a validation approach. As the project grows with more API routes, a consistent validation pattern (e.g., zod schema validation) would reduce bugs.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/api/auth/register/route.ts lines 11-44 — manual validation checks for each field. The email/phone format validation (lines 37-38) is notably weak compared to what a schema validator would provide.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed the fullstack-worker skill procedure correctly: read feature description, checked preconditions, wrote tests first (TDD), implemented API routes then UI, ran all checks (test/typecheck/lint), verified with agent-browser, then committed. Skill feedback confirms followedProcedure: true with no deviations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.followedProcedure is true, verification shows all 3 command checks passed (test, typecheck, lint), and 7 interactive browser checks were performed.

summary: The auth-system feature passes review. All 14 expected behaviors from the feature description are covered: login page with SAE-A Trading branding, Phone/Email tab switcher, password toggle, successful login redirect, wrong password error, empty field validation, register with new account, duplicate registration error, protected route redirect, and logged-in user redirect from auth pages. The implementation includes 9 new files (964 lines) with 18 tests covering API validation and UI component behavior. Non-blocking concerns include missing SessionProvider (not needed yet but will be for future features), permissive email/phone format validation, and user ID exposure in register response. Code quality is good with consistent style, proper error handling, and clear separation of concerns.
